Meeting April 14, 2026 
File: CO-05 
COUNCIL REPORT 
 
TO: 
MAYOR & COUNCILLORS 
 
FROM: 
GENERAL MANAGER CORPORATE SERVICES  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
APPOINTMENT OF CHIEF ELECTION OFFICER 
 
PURPOSE: 
To recommend the appointment of the Chief Election Officer for the 2026 
City of Burnaby General Local Election. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT Dennis Back be appointed Chief Election Officer for the 2026 City of 
Burnaby General Local Election. 
 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
Under Section 52 of the Local Government Act (LGA) and Section 35 of the School Act, 
general local elections for the offices of Mayor, Councillor, and School Trustee are held 
every four years on the third Saturday in October. The last general local election was held 
in 2022; the next is scheduled for Saturday, October 17, 2026. 
Section 58 of the LGA requires local governments to appoint a Chief Election Officer and 
a Deputy Chief Election Officer to conduct the election. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
On September 9, 2025, Council appointed Samantha Pellizzari as Chief Election Officer 
along with two Deputy Chief Election Officers for the 2026 City of Burnaby General Local 
Election. S. Pellizzari is currently on leave and is not anticipated to return prior to the 2026 
Local General Election on October 17, 2026. 
 
Alix Nay and Josh Comia continue to support the Election Office as Deputy Chief Election 
Officers. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
Following an exhaustive search for available and experienced contractors, Dennis Back 
is recommended for appointment as Chief Election Officer. It is necessary to appoint a 
new Chief Election Officer to ensure continuity in the election planning process and 
support the effective administration of the 2026 City of Burnaby General Local Election. 
Dennis Back worked in local and provincial government for many years and has held a 
variety of administrative and management positions, including time at the City of Burnaby 
as Municipal Clerk (Corporate Officer). 
29

Subject: Appointment of Chief Election Officer 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Not Applicable. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Funds for the services provided by Mr. Back as Chief Election Officer are included in the 
2026 Local General Election budget, funded by the Operating Surplus & Stabilization 
Reserve. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
Juli Halliwell 
General Manager Corporate Services 
ATTACHMENTS 
Not Applicable. 
REPORT CONTRIBUTORS 
This report was prepared by Blanka Zeinabova, Sr. Manager Legislative Services.

COUNCIL REPORT 
 
TO: 
MAYOR & COUNCILLORS 
 
FROM: 
CORPORATE OFFICER  
 
 
SUBJECT: 
CERTIFICATE OF SUFFICIENCY: NORTH ROAD BIA RENEWAL 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide results for the petitions submitted against the proposed North 
Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) renewal bylaw for a 5-year 
period. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Certificate of Sufficiency: North Road BIA Renewal” dated 
April 14, 2026 be received for information; 
THAT the Certificate of Sufficiency dated April 14, 2026, certified by the Corporate 
Officer and included as Attachment 1 to this report, be received; and 
THAT a copy of this report be sent to the President of the Burnaby North Road 
Business Improvement Association. 
 
1.0 EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
On February 24, 2026, Council authorized staff to work with the Burnaby North Road 
Business Improvement Association (the “Association”) towards the renewal of the 
Burnaby North Road Business Improvement Area (BNRBIA) for a period of five years, 
from April 1, 2026 to March 31, 2031. This report provides Council with the results of the 
Council Initiative petition process related to the proposed renewal of the BNRBIA and 
advises Council not to proceed with adoption of the proposed BNRBIA renewal bylaw. 
2.0  POLICY SECTION 
 
Under the Community Charter, the City can use one of two mechanisms to confirm 
support or objection to a business improvement area: 
• Section 212 - Petition “For” a Local Area Service 
• Section 213 - Local Area Service on Council Initiative — Subject to Petition 
“Against” a Local Area Service 
The process commonly followed by municipalities across BC for business improvement 
areas and the one that the City previously followed is the “Local Area Service on Council 
Initiative — Petition “Against” process. For the “Petition Against” process, the City mailed 
out notices to owners of all Class 5 (Light industrial) and Class 6 (Business) properties 
within the proposed BNRBIA boundary seeking to hear from those who are opposed to 
31

Subject: Certificate of Sufficiency: North Road BIA Renewal 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
renewal of the BNRBIA within a 30-day petition period. Council may not proceed with the 
BNRBIA if those signing as not in support of the BNRBIA represent at least 50% of the 
parcels in the BNRBIA and at least 50% of the assessed value of land and improvements 
in the BNRBIA. 
3.0 BACKGROUND 
On February 23, 2026, Council considered and gave three readings to the Burnaby 
Business Improvement Area (Burnaby North Road) Bylaw 2026, Bylaw No. 14797.  
The Bylaw defined the boundaries of the BIA and estimated the total local service tax that 
will be charged based on the current assessed value of land or improvements, or both, 
that fall within Class 5 (Light Industry) or Class 6 (Business and Other) properties within 
the BNRBIA boundary.  
Before the bylaw can be adopted, staff are required to undertake a 30-day petition 
process to determine opposition for the BNRBIA from all Class 5 and 6 property owners 
within the BNRBIA’s proposed boundaries. Notices and petition letters were sent to the 
property owners. Tenants of the subject properties were also sent notification letters. 
Owner notices and tenant letters included the estimated charges for the Local Area 
Service Tax based on completed assessment values, the boundaries of the BNRBIA, the 
petition submission deadline, as well as instructions on how to submit petitions and tenant 
opposition letters to the City. 
4.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
Under the Community Charter, the City can use one of two mechanisms to confirm 
support or objection to a business improvement area:  
• 
Section 212 - Petition “For” a Local Area Service 
• 
Section 213 - Local Area Service on Council Initiative — Petition “Against” a Local     
Area Service       
The process followed by all municipalities across BC for business improvement 
associations is the “Local Area Service on Council Initiative — Petition Against” under 
Section 213 of the Community Charter, despite the proposal for a BIA typically being 
initiated by the businesses within the BIA or the BIAs. 
For “petitions against” to be deemed sufficient, the petitions against the business 
improvement area must be signed by the owners of at least 50% of the parcels that would 
be subject to the local area tax for the proposed project, and the petitioners must be the 
owners of parcels that in total represent at least 50% of the assessed value of land and 
improvements that would be subject to the local service tax.  
The BNRBIA petition process opened on February 25, 2026, and closed on 
March 30, 2026, at 4:45 pm. During this time, staff under the direction of the Corporate 
Officer collected opposition petition letters received from the applicable property owners.   
32

Subject: Certificate of Sufficiency: North Road BIA Renewal 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 3 
 
While tenant letters and submissions do not form part of the official count and cannot be 
certified by the Corporate Officer, these submissions are available to Council and the 
public for information purposes and may be considered by Council when deciding 
whether to give final adoption to the BNRBIA Bylaw. 
4.1 Summary of Petitions Submitted by the Property Owners 
121 notice letters were mailed out to property owners, representing 120 separate folio 
numbers (due to multiple owners of the same property). In total, Legislative Services 
received 66 Notices of Opposition for 65 folio numbers (note: one letter contained four 
folio numbers).  
Additionally, Legislative Services received the following submissions after the 
submission deadline of 4:45pm on March 30, 2026: 
• one (1) notice letter received on March 30, 2026 at 4:52 pm 
• three (3) notice letters received on April 1, 2026 
• one (1) petition letter from Strata Corporation NW1901 with 50 signatures 
received on March 31, 2026 at 8:27 am (note: not part of mail out)

By the petition deadline of 4:45 pm on March 30, 2026, a total of 66 petitions opposing 
the BNRBIA renewal were received, representing 55% of the parcels that would be 
subject to the local service tax, and accounting for 52% of the total assessed value of 
land and improvements that would be subject to the local service tax, which meets the 
50% threshold requirement for sufficient petitions against.   
Accordingly, Council may not proceed with adoption of the Burnaby Business 
Improvement Area (North Road) Bylaw 2026, Bylaw No. 14797.  The Certificate of 
Sufficiency is provided as Attachment 1. It is recommended that a copy of this report 
be forwarded to the North Road Business Improvement Association advising them that 
the renewal was not successful.  
4.2 Statements in Oppositions from the Tenants 
Staff mailed out 264 unique letters to tenants, which represented a total of 74 folios. 
In total, Legislative Services received 19 unique tenant letters in opposition, 
representing 17 folios.  
33

Subject: Certificate of Sufficiency: North Road BIA Renewal 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 4 
 
4.3 Next steps 
Under subsections 213(6) and (7) of the Community Charter, if a council has been 
prevented from undertaking a local area service because of a sufficient petition 
against, the council must not propose the same service on its own initiative within a 
period of one year after the presentation of the petition unless the proposed local area 
service is varied from or less expensive than that originally proposed. Should the 
Association wish to make another attempt at renewing the BNRBIA under Section 213 
of the Community Charter (local area service on Council initiative — subject to petition 
against) within the next year, then the service must be varied from or less expensive 
than that originally proposed. The timing required (30-day petition process, plus 
required bylaw readings) for this process would mean that it could not be completed in 
time for the 2026 tax levy (mid-May).  
Staff would therefore recommend that if the Association makes another request to 
renew the BNRBIA, that Council not consider initiating another local area service on 
Council initiative for the BNRBIA until the fall, provided that the Association can 
demonstrate that there is sufficient owner support for the BNRBIA. Alternatively, the 
Association may, under Section 212 of the Community Charter, petition for a local area 
service. In order for a petition for a local area service under Section 212 of the 
Community Charter to be certified as sufficient and valid, the petition must be signed 
by the owners of at least 50% of the parcels that would be subject to the local service 
tax, and the persons signing must be the owners of parcels that in total represent at 
least 50% of the assessed value of land and improvements that would be subject to 
the local service tax. 
5.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Public notice was provided on the City’s Public Notice page as well as the City’s 
eNewsletter, in accordance with the City’s Public Notice Bylaw.  
Received owner and tenant opposition letters were available for public inspection in the 
Legislative Services department at City Hall for two weeks from March 30, 2026, until 
April 14, 2026. 
6.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Given that sufficient petitions in opposition to the renewal of the BNRBIA were received 
which prevents Council from adopting the Burnaby Business Improvement Area (Burnaby 
North Road) Bylaw, 2026, mill rates for the BRNBIA will not be included within the 
Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026, and the City will not undertake billing and collection of funds 
in 2026 for the BNRBIA. As a result, the Association will not receive grant funding for the 
period from April 1, 2026 to March 31, 2027. 
Respectfully submitted, 
Juli Halliwell, Corporate Officer

SUBJECT: 
BHA – SHAREHOLDER’S CORPORATE MAINTENANCE MATTERS 
FOR 2026 
 
PURPOSE: 
To obtain Council approval with respect to certain annual shareholder’s 
corporate maintenance matters for 2026 on behalf of the Burnaby 
Housing Authority Corporation (BHA). 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the City, as outlined in the report titled “BHA – Shareholder’s Corporate 
Maintenance Matters for 2026” dated April 14, 2026, as sole shareholder of the 
Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (BHA): and; 
(a) 
authorize the business of the annual general meeting of the BHA for 
the 2026 year to be transacted through an unanimous shareholders 
resolution;  
(b) 
select December 12, 2026 as the annual reference date for the BHA;  
(c) 
appoint KPMG as auditor of the BHA until the next annual general 
meeting of the BHA, and the directors of the BHA be authorized to 
determine the remuneration payable to the auditor, and to give the 
auditor notice of appointment as required by the Business 
Corporations Act (British Columbia);  
(d) 
receive the BHA’s financial statements for the period ending 
December 31, 2025; and 
(e) 
appoint the individuals identified in section 3.3 as directors of the BHA 
in accordance with the terms set out in section. 
THAT the Shareholder Representative be directed to execute such shareholders 
resolutions as necessary to give effect to the foregoing resolutions. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
Shareholder’s corporate maintenance matters outlined in this report aligns with City 
policies, including:  
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022);  
• HOME: Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021);  
• Burnaby Housing Needs Report (2021); 
• Mayor's Task Force on Community Housing Final Report (2019);  
• Burnaby Social Sustainability Strategy (2011);  
• Burnaby Economic Development Strategy (2007); and 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025). 
 
 
37

Subject: BHA –Shareholder’s Corporate Maintenance Matters for 2026 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (BHA) was incorporated on 
June 14, 2024, with the City as its sole shareholder. Council appointed the City’s 
Chief Administrative Officer as the Shareholder Representative for the BHA to, 
among other things, pass Shareholder Resolutions and do all other acts necessary 
for the City to carry out and exercise its rights as the shareholder of the Company as 
directed by Council.  
 
Following incorporation of the BHA, the Shareholder Representative, by a 
Shareholder Resolution, appointed the following individuals to the board of directors, 
on an interim basis, from August 20, 2024 until the BHA’s first AGM: 
 
1. Mike Hurley 
2. Alison Gu 
3. Daniel Tetrault 
4. Noreen Kassam 
5. Stephani Baker 
6. Ian Cullis 
7. Lilian Kan 
8. Aubrey Kelly 
9. Michael Leroux 
10. Andrew Tong 
 
At the November 25, 2025 Council meeting, Council authorized the City, as sole 
shareholder of the BHA, to: 
• waive the holding of a BHA’s first AGM in 2025;  
• select December 12, 2025 as the annual reference date for the BHA; and 
• waive the production and publication of financial statements by the BHA for 
the 2024 fiscal year within 120 days of the end of the 2024 fiscal year, subject 
to the financial statements for the 2024 fiscal year being audited at the end of 
the fiscal year 2025, and the first set of audited financial statements being 
provided to the City within 60 days of the 2025 fiscal year end. 
 
The purpose of this report is to obtain Council authorization to: 
Transact all of the business that is required to be transacted at the BHA’s 2026 AGM by 
a Shareholder Resolution, as follows: 
• to select the annual reference date for the BHA; 
• to acknowledge receipt of the BHA’s financial statements for the financial period 
ending December 31, 2025; 
• to appoint an auditor for the BHA; 
• to appoint to the BHA Board, five Independent Directors for a two-year term, and 
the three City Directors and an Independent Director for a three-year term; and 
• direct the Shareholder Representative to execute such shareholders resolutions as 
necessary to give effect to the foregoing resolutions. 
 
 
38

Subject: BHA –Shareholder’s Corporate Maintenance Matters for 2026 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 3 
 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1 Shareholder Resolutions in Lieu of 2026 AGM  
Under section 182(1) of the Business Corporations Act (BCA), a company is 
required to hold an AGM at least once in each calendar year and not more than 15 
months after the annual reference date for the preceding calendar year, unless the 
shareholders unanimously resolve to waive it or unanimously pass written 
resolutions dealing with all business normally conducted at the AGM.  
When written resolutions are passed in place of holding an AGM, the shareholder 
must select an annual reference date – representing the date the AGM would 
otherwise have taken place – to maintain statutory continuity. The City, as sole 
shareholder of the BHA, may pass such resolutions to transact the business of the 
AGM in lieu of holding an AGM. 
3.2 Annual Reference Date 
Under the BCA, the Annual Reference Date (“ARD”) is defined as the date, 
selected by shareholders, that the Annual General Meeting is held. If the 
shareholders, by an unanimous resolution, consent to all of the business required 
to be transacted at an AGM, then then the shareholders must select as the 
company’s ARD a date that would be appropriate for the holding of the AGM that 
is being waived. The BHA has requested that the City set December 12, 2026 as 
the annual reference date. 
 
3.3 Appointment of Directors 
Under s.13.1 of the BHA’s Articles, at every AGM, the City, as sole shareholder, will 
appoint a board of directors for the BHA.  Under the BHA Articles, the BHA Board 
consists of 10 directors, four of which are affiliated with the City (“City Directors”) 
and six of which are independent of the City and individuals with experience and 
expertise in various aspects of housing and real estate development (“Independent 
Directors”).   
To ensure the entire Board does not turnover at the same time, the BHA Articles 
stagger the initial terms of the directors with five Independent Directors serving an 
initial two-year term and the four City Directors and one Independent Director 
serving an initial three-year term. At every succeeding AGM, those directors whose 
term on the Board is set to expire will be re-appointed or replaced for subsequent 
three-year terms. The Articles limit any director from serving more than two 
consecutive terms or a maximum of six consecutive years to ensure a diversity of 
perspectives on the BHA Board. The Articles permit past directors to be eligible for 
reappointment after an absence of at least one year.  
The current board of directors was appointed by the City, as sole shareholder, on 
an interim basis until the BHA’s first AGM.  As the City waived the 2025 AGM, the 
interim directors continued as directors. As part of the business to be conducted at 
the 2026 AGM, the City, as sole shareholder, must appoint directors to the BHA 
Board.  It is recommended that Council appoint the current interim directors as 
directors to the BHA Board for either a two- or three-year term as indicated below:

Not Applicable.

Respectfully submitted, 
 
Ed Kozak, General Manager Planning and Development and Bob Klimek, Acting Chief 
Financial Officer

None.

Meeting April 14, 2026 
File: CS-05 
COUNCIL REPORT 
 
TO: 
MAYOR & COUNCILLORS 
FROM: 
DEPUTY CHIEF ADMINISTRATIVE OFFICER AND CHIEF FINANCIAL 
OFFICER 
 
SUBJECT: 
BHA – 2025 FINANCAL STATEMENTS AND 2026 OPERATING 
BUDGET 
 
PURPOSE: 
To present the Burnaby Housing Authority’s (BHA) 2025 Annual Financial 
Statements and 2026 Annual Operating Budget. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “BHA – 2025 Financial Statements and 2026 Operating 
Budget”, dated for April 14, 2026, be received for information; 
THAT the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation’s (BHA) 2025 Annual Financial 
Statements, which includes the audited Consolidated Financial Statements, for the 
period from June 14, 2024 to December 31, 2025 be received; and 
THAT the BHA’s 2026 Annual Operating Budget be received. 
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
This report presents the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation’s (BHA) 2025 Audited 
Financial Statements and 2026 Operating Budget, as required under Partnering 
Agreement No. 1, to support Council’s consideration of Year 2 operating funding.  The 
report confirms a clean audit opinion, compliance with public-sector accounting 
standards, and the establishment of a sound financial and organizational foundation while 
advancing the BHA’s initial housing projects at 10th Avenue and Newcombe Street and 
Marine Drive (Edmonds). Receipt of this information satisfies the conditions for the City 
to provide $2.00 million in 2026 operating funding, supporting the ongoing delivery of 
non-market and market housing aligned with the City’s strategic housing objectives. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
Receipt of the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation’s (BHA) 2025 Audited Financial 
Statements and 2026 Operating Budget to facilitate the development and administration 
of non-market and market housing by the BHA in Burnaby on behalf of the City aligns with 
City policies, including:  
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022);  
• HOME: Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021);  
• Burnaby Housing Needs Report (2021); 
• Mayor's Task Force on Community Housing Final Report (2019);  
• Burnaby Social Sustainability Strategy (2011);  
• Burnaby Economic Development Strategy (2007); and 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025). 
 
 
41

Subject: BHA – 2025 Financial Statements and 2026 Operating Budget 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (BHA) is a wholly owned subsidiary of the 
City of Burnaby, incorporated on June 14, 2024, as a local government corporation under 
the BC Business Corporations Act. The BHA was established to support the City’s 
strategic objective of increasing the supply of non-market and market housing. Its 
mandate is to invest in, develop, acquire, hold, and manage land and buildings to deliver 
housing across a range of affordability levels. The BHA prepares its financial statements 
in accordance with Canadian Public Sector Accounting Standards (PSAS). 
 
In accordance with the Community Charter, the City and the BHA must enter into a 
partnering agreement before the City can provide assistance. In 2025, the City and the 
BHA executed two such agreements, defining the assistance to be provided and the 
housing-related services to be delivered in return. On February 11, 2025, Council 
approved the first partnering agreement (PA No. 1), which provides $2,000,000 in annual 
operating funding for a five-year period. Release of Year 2 operating funding is contingent 
on Council’s receipt and approval of the BHA’s Final Strategic Plan and receipt of the 
BHA’s 2025 Financial Statements and updated 2026 Operating Budget. 
Council approved the BHA’s 2026-2030 Final Strategic Plan at its open meeting on March 
24, 2026. 
The purpose of this report is to present the BHA’s 2025 Annual Financial Statements for 
the period from June 14, 2024 to fiscal year ended December 31, 2025, being its first full 
year of operations, and its 2026 Operating Budget to City Council. 
The BHA’s 2025 Annual Financial Statements (Attachment 1) were approved by the BHA 
Board on February 26, 2026. The BHA’s 2026 Operating Budget (Attachment 2) was 
approved by the BHA Board on November 18, 2025. 
Representatives from the BHA will attend the Council Meeting to respond to Council’s 
questions about the 2025 Financial Statements and the updated 2026 Operating Budget. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1  Independent Auditor’s Report  
The BHA’s independent auditor, KPMG, issued a clean, unmodified opinion on the 
Authority’s inaugural financial statements for the period June 14, 2024 to December 
31, 2025.  The auditor confirmed that the statements fairly present the BHA’s financial 
position, results of operations, changes in net financial assets, and cash flows in 
accordance with Canadian Public Sector Accounting Standards. This unqualified 
conclusion demonstrates that the BHA’s first reporting period was presented in a 
transparent, accurate, and compliant manner, with no matters requiring modification 
of the auditor’s opinion. 
 
 
42

Subject: BHA – 2025 Financial Statements and 2026 Operating Budget 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 3 
 
3.2  Financial Reporting  
Accounting Framework 
The BHA prepares its financial statements in accordance with Canadian Public Sector 
Accounting Standards (PSAS) and follows full accrual accounting. Statements were 
prepared on a going concern basis, supported by ongoing financial contributions from 
the City. 
Overall Financial Summary 
As of December 31, 2025, the BHA reported an accumulated surplus of $7.52 million, 
made up of $5.55 million in tangible capital assets and $1.97 million in operating 
surplus. Cash holdings totalled $31.75 million, including a $25 million GIC maturing in 
November 2026.  The BHA reported receivables of $326,073 and payables of 
$619,696. 
From incorporation to year-end, the BHA generated $8.21 million in revenue, primarily 
from City grants, and incurred $683,684 in expenses, resulting in a $7.52 million 
annual surplus.   For its initial year of operations, the BHA incurred expenses equal to 
roughly 28% of its total operating budget, because core staff positions (e.g. CEO, 
Financial Controller).  Staff were still being hired, and operational activities were just 
starting up. 
Net financial assets increased by $1.95 million, reflecting the annual surplus offset by 
$5.55 million in capital asset acquisitions and minor changes in non-financial assets. 
Cash flows were strong, with $37.30 million provided by operating activities, mainly 
due to an annual surplus of $7.52 million and an increase of $29.50 million in deferred 
revenue, and $5.55 million used for capital purchases, yielding a net cash increase of 
$31.75 million from zero at incorporation. 
City Funding and Related Party Transactions 
Between incorporation and the 2025 year-end, the BHA received $37.48 million in 
grants from the City, which remain its primary funding source. These include startup, 
operating, and capital grants intended to support establishment costs, annual 
operating requirements, and project development activities. 
The City also provided administrative services during initial setup, incurred prior to the 
BHA hiring its current employees, and the BHA made payments for development 
related fees. The BHA remains economically dependent on the City for its ongoing 
operational and capital needs. 
Deferred Revenue and Project Funding 
Deferred revenue totaled $29.51 million at yearend, representing unspent restricted 
contributions allocated to two major development projects. Revenue is recognized as 
project related conditions and performance obligations are met.  Project specific 
allocations include: 
• 
10th Avenue & Newcombe Street Project: $23.21 million 
• 
Marine Drive (Edmonds) Project: $6.30 million 
These amounts reflect the portion of City funding not yet recognized as revenue. 
43

Subject: BHA – 2025 Financial Statements and 2026 Operating Budget 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 4 
 
Capital Projects and Asset Development 
Tangible capital assets amounted to $5.55 million, primarily representing assets under 
development for the BHA’s two major housing initiatives. Assets under development 
are capitalized until ready for use, and no impairments or asset retirement obligations 
were identified. 
The City’s major housing developments include the 10th Avenue & Newcombe Street 
project, featuring two six-storey rental buildings with 204 units offering a mix of 
affordable and market homes with shared amenities, and the Marine Drive (Edmonds) 
development, which will provide 183 non-market and market rental townhomes in two- 
and three-bedroom layouts, complemented by outdoor amenity spaces. 
Financial Resilience and Risk Management  
The BHA holds multi-year contractual obligations for services and benefits from 
Partnering Agreements with the City, which provide ongoing operating and capital 
funding subject to performance criteria. 
The BHA’s exposure to financial risk is assessed as low. Conservative investment 
practices minimize credit and market risk, while liquidity risk is mitigated through 
regular cash flow monitoring and secure funding commitments from the City. 
Operating Highlights  
In its inaugural year, the BHA achieved several significant milestones. The BHA

PURPOSE: 
To propose amendments to the Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw and the 
Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw to implement the E-Bike Share Pilot 
Program and address the provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program 
within the City. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the City Solicitor be authorized to bring forward amendments to the 
following bylaws to implement the E-Bike Share Pilot Program and address the 
provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program: 
(a) 
Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961; 
(b)

1.0 POLICY SECTION 
This report responds to the City of Burnaby’s Corporate Strategic Plan and goals for a 
connected and dynamic community and is aligned with our Council-adopted policies, 
plans, and strategies within the Burnaby Transportation Plan, Connecting Burnaby. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
The City of Burnaby is preparing to launch an E-Bike Share Pilot Program as part of 
ongoing efforts to expand sustainable transportation options, reduce dependence on 
personal vehicles, and advance municipal transportation goals. The E-Bike Share Pilot 
Program will introduce a convenient, low-emission option for short distance trips, 
helping residents travel with ease without relying on personal vehicles. 
This initiative builds on the City’s participation in the provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot 
Program, which came into effect on January 1, 2026, allowing residents to operate 
privately owned electric kick scooters in Burnaby. Together, these programs diversify 
65

Subject: Proposed Bylaw Amendments to Address E-Bike Share Pilot Program and Provincial Electric 
Kick Scooter Pilot Program 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
transportation options, promote healthier and more active travel, and reduce traffic 
congestion and greenhouse gas emissions. 
Integrating both e-bikes and e-scooters into Burnaby’s mobility network will provide 
residents with more flexible, affordable, and environmentally friendly ways to travel 
throughout the community. 
Enforcement of the operation and use of personal and shared e-bikes and electric kick 
scooters will primarily fall under the jurisdiction of law enforcement (i.e. the Burnaby 
RCMP), as these are considered moving vehicle violations and infractions are regulated 
under the Motor Vehicle Act (MVA). 
This report presents several amendments to the Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw, 
Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw and Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw to 
address the following on City streets and parks: 
• the Burnaby E-Bike Share Pilot Program; and 
• the provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program.

In order to implement the proposed E-Bike Share Pilot Program and address the 
provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program, amendments to the following bylaws are 
required: 
• Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961 
• Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979 
• Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 
The proposed amendments to these bylaws are set out in Attachment 1 of this report 
and key changes are described below: 
Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 
• Introduce key definitions such as Cycles, Motor Assisted Cycles, Electric Kick 
Scooters, Shared Electric-Bikes, and Shared Micromobility Devices, and to 
establish rules for their use in the public right-of-way. 
• Authorize the City to designate Shared Micromobility Parking Zones, regulate 
fleet deployment, parking, and device speed, and clarify operator 
responsibilities. 
Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 
• Introduce key definitions for Cycles, Motor Assisted Cycles, and Electric Kick 
Scooters.

• Amend to provide that no person may use a Cycle, Motor Assisted Cycle, or 
Electric Kick Scooter within a park except in areas specifically designated for 
such use.  
Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 
• Amend violation provision for Section 44 of the Parks Regulation Bylaw to 
add driving or riding a Cycle, Motor Assisted Cycle or Electric Kick Scooter in 
an area not designated for such use. As explained above, violations of e-
bikes and electric kick scooters are considered moving vehicle offences and 
enforced by the RCMP under the Motor Vehicle Act (MVA). 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Staff will develop targeted communication and public education materials to support the 
launch of the E-Bike Share Pilot Program and the City’s continued participation in the 
provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program. The focus will be on encouraging the 
safe and lawful use of e-bikes and privately owned electric kick scooters, and on raising 
awareness of new bylaw requirements and designated riding and parking areas. 
Communications may include updates to the City’s website, social media content, and 
printed materials.

The E-Bike Share Pilot Program will generate revenue from the number of devices 
operating in the City and the usage of the devices. The new revenue will contribute to 
the E-Bike Share Pilot Program’s operational and maintenance costs for the City.

1.0  POLICY SECTION 
The City of Burnaby Housing Target Progress Report aligns with the provincial Housing 
Supply Act (2023).  
Additionally, this report aligns with the following City policies and strategies:  
• HOME: Burnaby’s Housing and Homelessness Strategy (2021); 
• Burnaby’s Housing Needs Report (2021, updated 2024); and 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025). 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
The Housing Supply Act came into effect in Spring 2023. It enables the Province to 
establish housing targets for municipalities across British Columbia as part of a broader 
legislative framework intended to address the housing crisis. Housing targets have been 
issued to municipalities on a staggered basis, and the City of Burnaby, as part of Group 
4, received its Housing Target Order on August 18, 2025 (see Attachment 1).  
The Order sets a requirement for the City to deliver 10,240 net new housing units 
between September 1, 2025, and August 31, 2030, which is derived based on meeting 
75% of the total Provincial Housing Needs Estimate for the City of Burnaby. The net new 
units are measured cumulatively with each reporting period, as illustrated in Table 1.

Under the Housing Supply Act, municipalities subject to a Housing Target Order must 
submit regular progress reports to the Province. For Burnaby, the following progress 
reports are mandated over the five‑year period: 
 
• Year 1a Report: September 1, 2025 – February 28, 2026 (6-month interim report); 
• Year 1b Report: September 1, 2025 – August 31, 2026; 
• Year 2 Report: September 1, 2026 – August 31, 2027; 
• Year 3 Report: September 1, 2027 – August 31, 2028;  
• Year 4 Report: September 1, 2028 – August 31, 2029; and 
• Year 5 Report: September 1, 2029 – August 31, 2030. 
 
The first six-month interim progress report is intended to provide the Province with an 
early indication of a municipality’s ability to meet or make progress toward achieving its 
housing targets. The report further describes actions taken by the municipality to meet its 
housing targets, such as adoption of policies, plans, and bylaws that address housing 
needs.  
Each progress report must be received in a meeting that is open to the public and by 
Council resolution within 45 days following the end of its respective reporting period, 
which for Burnaby is April 14, 2026, for the current reporting period. Municipalities must 
subsequently submit their progress report to the Province as soon as is practicable.  
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
Progress Towards Provincial Target  
For the 6-month interim reporting period of September 1, 2025, to February 28, 2026, 
approximately 910 net new housing units have been completed within Burnaby.  
While the City is currently on track to meet its Year 1 Housing Target Order requirements, 
current economic conditions are creating uncertainty in the construction and delivery of 
new housing units with development starts having slowed across British Columbia. The 
City has streamlined its development processes to facilitate timely reviews and approvals 
of new housing project. However, with housing units mainly constructed by the private 
market, the City ultimately does not control when housing will be completed, making it 
difficult for the City to control whether it can meet its Housing Target Order. 
In addition, the recent announcement to pause provincial funding programs for non-
market housing programs, including the Community Housing Fund (CHF), will have an 
73

Subject: Provincial Housing Target Order 6-Month Interim Progress Report 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 3 
 
impact on municipalities, including the City of Burnaby, in delivering new housing units, 
especially affordable housing. The program has historically supported a broad spectrum 
of affordable, purpose-built rental housing, including projects with deep levels of subsidy, 
such as Rent-Geared-to-Income (RGI) and shelter-rate units. This program has been a 
key mechanism for achieving the Province's long-term affordable housing supply targets, 
and many non-profit housing providers are reliant on this funding to move projects from 
planning to delivery. With no new funding anticipated for the foreseeable future, a 
number of projects that had anticipated delivering deeply affordable rental units, such as 
Hall Towers which has 331 proposed units in Edmonds, are now experiencing 
uncertainty regarding their timelines and viability.  
Burnaby’s Housing Target Progress Report Form is provided in Attachment 2. This 
provides a comprehensive summary of the housing units delivered for this six-month 
interim reporting period, along with units currently in the development pipeline. It also 
provides additional information on the housing initiatives undertaken by the City in the 
past 12 months. 
4.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
The Housing Target Progress Report Form will be posted on the City’s website and 
submitted to the Province to satisfy Housing Target Order requirements. The next 
provincial reporting deadline is August 31, 2026, and thereafter annually until 2030. 
5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
The completion and submission of the Housing Target Progress Report does not have a 
financial impact on the City. However, the delivery of housing units to meet the Housing 
Target Order placed on Burnaby, as well as the associated housing policies, plans and 
strategies needed to facilitate the delivery of new housing, will require resources, 
especially in relation to non-market housing in the form of potential grants, fee waivers 
and staff support. In light of the funding pause from the provincial government on its 
affordable housing funding program, it will be challenging for the City to achieve the 
Housing Target Order, particularly affordable units, without funding support from other 
orders of government. As such, it is finally noted that failure to achieve the Housing 
Target Order (non-market guideline) is a reflection of the Province’s inability to meet it’s 
own commitments, and not on the City’s ability to deliver such housing.

PURPOSE:

RECOMMENDATION

described in the report titled “Building Benchmark BC,” dated April 14, 2026, with

1.0  POLICY SECTION 
The City of Burnaby’s partnership with Building Benchmark BC aligns with the following 
City plans and policies:  
• Environmental Sustainability Strategy (2016); 
• Community Energy and Emissions Plan (2016); 
• Climate Action Framework (2020); 
• City Energy Strategy (2020); 
• Zero Emission Building Retrofit Strategy (2022); and, 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025). 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
Building Benchmark BC (BBBC) is a voluntary energy benchmarking and disclosure 
program launched in 2020 that has grown to involve more than 1,900 buildings and 
more than 90 organizations across British Columbia. It continues to be a collaborative 
initiative spearheaded by local and regional governments, BC Hydro and the University 
of British Columbia, working with Open Technologies, to promote climate change 
solutions in the built environment through collective action and scalable solutions for 
informed decision-making. 
BBBC allows building owners and managers to measure, track and compare their 
buildings energy use and carbon emissions against similar properties, providing insights 
for operational improvements and retrofit opportunities. The participating jurisdictions 
and partners also continue to explore the role of building energy benchmarking and 
disclosure within a broader framework of climate regulations, policies, and incentives.   
The City of Burnaby has been a local government partner of BBBC since its inception in 
2020. Council last received a report on BBBC in 2023. Since that time, the City has 
continued as a local government partner by promoting the program to the public, 
leading by example by including 30 civic buildings in the program and, alongside other 
local government partners, making an annual funding contribution to support technical 
components, delivery and administration of the program. 
90

3.1  BBBC Annual Program Report 
Annual program reports, information about the program and the link to the public 
disclosure map can be found at BBBC’s website - www.buildingbenchmarkbc.ca.   
BBBC Annual Program Report 2024 (Year 5) is included as Attachment 1 for 
information. Positive trends and outcomes from the report include:  
• Overall trend of emissions reduction based on average emissions intensity of 
participating buildings from Years 1 to 5;  
• Growth in participating buildings to 1,919 in Year 5 (from 765 in Year 1) and 15 
million square feet of floor space; in Burnaby, participating buildings and square 
footage have more than tripled since Year 1; and 
• Award for Best Improvement of Energy Performance given to Lochdale 
Elementary School (Burnaby Schools – School District 41). 
The annual score cards prepared for Burnaby civic buildings have shown notable 
improvements in building performance for buildings as they have undergone energy 
and/or emissions retrofits.  
The Year 6 report is not yet available.   
3.2  BBBC Year 7 
2026 will be Year 7 of BBBC. Buildings already in the program will receive information 
about their energy use and emissions from the previous calendar year, benchmarked 
against other similar buildings for comparison. Owners and managers of buildings 
recruited to join BBBC for the first time will share their building’s energy use for the 
current year. BBBC will continue to advocate for provincial leadership to develop 
mandatory energy reporting and benchmarking programs for existing buildings, similar to 
other jurisdictions in Canada, the USA and around world. In the meantime, BBBC members 
City of Victoria, District of Saanich and the Capital Regional District (CRD) are advancing 
their own regional energy reporting program for large buildings beginning in 2026. 
3.3  Burnaby Participation in BBBC Year 7 
For Burnaby, continuing our partnership with BBBC aligns with the City Energy Strategy 
and energy management of existing buildings to improve efficiency and low carbon 
performance. The partnership also supports the Climate Action Framework and Zero 
Emission Building Retrofit Strategy, specifically Actions 3.4 and 4.1 that focus on 
advancing policy and regulation to require energy reporting, similar to the program in 
the CRD region, and advocating for leadership from regional and provincial 
governments. Through the City’s continued partnership with BBBC, enrolled privately 
and publicly owned buildings in Burnaby have access to free energy-use and emissions 
data to inform the retrofit decision-making and strategic energy management needed to 
reducing building emissions from existing buildings.  
With Council authorization of this report, the City will continue its participation in Building

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY 
This recommendation is to approve a contract award in the amount of $1,354,463 including tax 
to Echelon Creative Inc. for this development project. 
The project will demonstrate the feasibility and cost effectiveness of standardized modular 
construction for small scale multi-unit housing in low density neighbourhoods. This pilot project 
forms part of a broader initiative supported by Modular BC and the National Research Council 
to advance low-carbon construction methods and scalable housing solutions.  The anticipated 
project delivery is approximately 6 – 8 months and typically, modular housing has an expected 
useful life of about 50 to 60 years. With proper maintenance and periodic upgrades, the lifespan 
can extend beyond 80 years.

fabrication, delivery, installation and coordination of trades to complete the modular building.

2.0 BACKGROUND 
The City of Burnaby is participating in a modular housing pilot initiative intended to evaluate 
the use of standardized factory-built construction for small-scale, multi-unit housing 
developments. The project site is located at 8315 14th Avenue. 
The pilot project is being undertaken in collaboration with Modular BC and forms part of the 
National Research Council’s Platform to Decarbonize the Construction Sector at Scale. 
The proposed building will include:

• Four residential units 
• Three storeys 
• One accessible dwelling unit 
• Private outdoor space for each unit 
The pilot will provide data and lessons learned that can inform future modular housing 
developments within Burnaby and across the province. 
Architectural drawings have been prepared and the design generally complies with Burnaby 
zoning requirements. Preliminary geotechnical review indicates the site is suitable for 
conventional foundation systems.

planning, operating practices, and capital investment priority setting, as 
discussed at the Financial Management Committee meeting held on March 17, 
2026, be approved; and  
 
THAT the reviewer liaise with the Financial Management Committee and deliver 
findings and recommendations directly to Council.

The Financial Management Committee, at its meeting held on March 17, 2026, received 
and adopted a Notice of Motion from Chair Dhaliwal seeking Council approval to 
engage an external reviewer to help safeguard the City’s assets and financial standing,

businesses and residents. Chair Dhaliwal noted that while the City’s financial position 
remains strong, sustaining the moderate tax increases of recent years is becoming 
increasingly challenging due to a sharp decline in density bonus revenues and 
significantly higher procurement costs.

MEMBERS OF COUNCIL

MAYOR MIKE HURLEY 
SUBJECT: 
UPDATE ON MAYOR’S TASK FORCE ON BURNABY ECONOMIC 
GROWTH AND RESILIENCE 
 
PURPOSE: 
To update members of Council on the progress of the Mayor’s Task 
Force on Burnaby Economic Growth and Resilience.  
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Update on Mayor’s Task Force on Burnaby Economic 
Growth and Resilience” dated April 14, 2026, be received for information. 
1.0 POLICY SECTION 
In accordance with the Community Charter (Division 4, Section 142) ‘Select Committees 
of Council’, Council may establish and appoint a select committee to consider or inquire 
into any matter and to report its findings and opinion to Council. 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
On September 9, 2025, Council approved the establishment of the Mayor’s Task Force 
on Burnaby Economic Growth and Resilience (the “Task Force”), including its Terms of 
Reference, as a select committee of Council. The Task Force was created to bring 
together a cross‑section of business and community leaders to explore Burnaby’s 
economic strengths and challenges and to develop informed, actionable 
recommendations to support long‑term economic growth and resilience. 
Task Force members were appointed by Council on December 9, 2025. The Task Force 
commenced its work in January 2026 and has been meeting through a series of 
bi‑weekly, facilitator‑supported workshops, to discuss and identify emerging themes 
through structured dialogue. 
This report provides Council with an interim update on the Task Force’s progress at the 
mid‑point of its work program, prior to the completion of its deliberations and the 
development of formal recommendations for Council’s consideration. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
3.1 Workshop series  
The Task Force commenced its work on January 8, 2026 and has met five times 
through facilitator-supported workshops to date. Workshops generally begin with an 
introductory discussion to frame the session topic, followed by presentations from guest 
174

Subject: Update on Mayor’s Task Force on Burnaby Economic Growth and Resilience 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
lecturers to provide insight into the broader economic landscape. Members then 
participate in facilitated workshop sessions guided by a series of focused questions. 
Guest presentations introduced key themes, including economic development in the 
municipal, regional and national context, as well as economic resilience and inclusive 
economic practices. Building on these presentations, members engaged in facilitated 
discussions to examine how these themes apply locally in Burnaby and to identify 
potential areas of strategic focus.  
A key milestone was reached during the most recent workshop on March 5, 2026, with 
the identification of strategic priorities intended to guide the development of 
recommendations to Council. Following this milestone, the Task Force paused its 
workshop meetings to allow for validation of the identified strategic priorities through 
targeted engagement, including surveys and interviews with the business community 
and key industry stakeholders who were not present at the workshop. The Task Force is 
scheduled to reconvene on April 16, 2026 to continue its work. 
3.2 Emerging themes 
Discussions emphasized the importance of creating the right conditions for businesses 
in sectors where Burnaby already demonstrates strength, as well as supporting 
businesses through their full life cycle, from start‑up to expansion and retention.  
Members also discussed the role the City could play in convening partners and aligning 
efforts to foster a more coordinated and collaborative economic ecosystem.  
Given Burnaby’s position as one of the most diverse cities in Western Canada, diversity 
was recognized as a key economic asset, with a focus on reducing barriers and 
expanding access for underrepresented groups. There was also recognition for the 
need to protect and strengthen the foundations of the local economy through resilient 
infrastructure and emergency preparedness. Collectively, these discussions informed 
the development of strategic priorities to be validated with the business community and 
key stakeholder groups. 
3.3 Next Steps 
At the time of writing, survey results are being compiled and analyzed. The Task Force 
will reconvene for three additional sessions between April 16 to end of May 2026 to 
confirm the strategic priorities and develop actionable recommendations for 
presentation to Council on June 23, 2026. 
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Engagement with the business community was undertaken through a survey and 10 
targeted stakeholder interviews conducted between March 13 and April 2, 2026.  This 
engagement was intended to inform and refine the draft strategic priorities. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
There are no financial considerations associated with this report.

SUBJECT: 
AMENITY PIER COVENANT – DEVELOPER ELECTION TO FORFEIT 
SECURITY 
 
PURPOSE: 
To provide Council with information regarding the owner’s election under 
a Section 219 Covenant registered on title at 8351 Fraser Reach Court 
to forfeit the posted security and not proceed with construction of the 
public amenity pier contemplated under the Riverbend Business Park 
rezoning approvals, and the City’s holding of the forfeited security 
pending future Council direction. 
 
RECOMMENDATION 
THAT the report titled “Amenity Pier Covenant – Developer Election to Forfeit 
Security” dated April 14, 2026, be received for information.  
1.0  POLICY SECTION 
The following report aligns with provincial regulation and municipal bylaws and policies, 
including: 
• Corporate Strategic Plan (2022); 
• Burnaby 2050 Official Community Plan (2025); and, 
• Economic Development Strategy (2007). 
2.0  BACKGROUND 
In 2016, Council approved REZ#12-18, which established a multi-phased Conceptual 
Master Plan and design guidelines for Oxford Properties’ Riverbend Business Park, 
located at 8211, 8220, 8340, and 8351 Fraser Reach Court (see Attachment 1 – 
Sketch #1). The Conceptual Plan included a detailed public open space concept that 
centered around the site’s adjacency to the Fraser River, with the goal of providing 
numerous points of access to the foreshore and an amenity pier on the Fraser River. 
One of the requirements established under the Riverbend Business Park Master Plan, 
and two site-specific rezoning applications for the business park (REZ#16-50 and 
REZ#18-33), was the provision of a public amenity pier in the Fraser River.  The pier 
was intended to connect to and complement the waterfront trail located within a 
statutory right-of-way, which in turn links to the broader Burnaby Fraser Foreshore Park 
and accommodates both pedestrians and cyclists. While a portion of the amenity pier 
was to be located on 8340 Fraser Reach Court, the majority was proposed to be located 
177

Subject: Amenity Pier Covenant – Developer Election to Forfeit Security 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 2 
 
within a water lot owned by the Province. Construction and operation within this area 
required a Crown Land Tenure from the Province in the form of a Nominal Rent Tenure 
(NRT) Lease. 
As part of the rezoning approval for REZ#18-33, a Section 219 Covenant was 
registered on title, restricting Final Occupancy of the new two-storey light industrial 
building at 8351 Fraser Reach Court until the identified amenity pier was constructed. 
However, because Oxford Properties wished to obtain occupancy of their approved 
buildings prior to satisfying the conditions set out in that Section 219 Covenant, a new 
Section 219 Covenant was registered against 8351 Fraser Reach Court. The covenant 
provides that if the owner is unable to obtain the required Crown Land Tenure, the 
owner may elect to forfeit the security in lieu of constructing the amenity pier. The 
covenant also provides that the City may use the forfeited security toward construction 
of the pier or an alternative public amenity along the Fraser Foreshore or elsewhere in 
the City. 
On December 13, 2021, Council authorized staff to submit the required provincial 
applications for the NRT Lease and to prepare a Construction, Operation and 
Maintenance Agreement with the property owner. On September 9th, 2025, Council 
received an information report that provided an update on the public amenity pier, 
including that it was anticipated that, subject to approval of the NRT Lease and 
completion of relevant City applications and processes, construction could potentially 
begin in late 2025. 
3.0  GENERAL INFORMATION 
Since that report was received by Council, Oxford Properties continued to work with the 
City and Province to develop plans for the amenity pier and obtain the required Crown 
Land Tenure from the Province. On December 19th, 2025, the property owner advised 
the City that it had been unable to secure the required Crown Land Tenure from the 
Province and has exercised its option under the covenant to forfeit the posted security 
in lieu of proceeding with construction of the amenity pier. In accordance with the terms 
of the Section 219 Covenant, security in the amount of $1,363,500 will be forfeited to 
the City, as acknowledged by the owner, and per 3.1(e) of the covenant, the “City may 
use the Security to secure completion of all or a portion of the design, construction, 
installation and completion of the Amenity Pier Works or an alternative amenity as 
deemed appropriate by the City in an alternate location along the Fraser Foreshore or 
elsewhere within the City of Burnaby.” 
Under the terms of the Section 219 Covenant, where the owner elects to forfeit the 
posted security in lieu of constructing the amenity pier, the City is required to discharge 
the covenant from title once the forfeited security has been received. Planning will 
provide instructions to the Legal Department to discharge the covenant accordingly. 
Retention of the $1,363,500 security ensures that funds originally secured to deliver a 
public amenity remain available to support future public amenity improvements 
consistent with the intent of the covenant. The owner has advised that their preference 
178

Subject: Amenity Pier Covenant – Developer Election to Forfeit Security 
Open Council Meeting April 14, 2026 ........................................... Page 3 
 
is to provide payment in the amount of $1,363,500 and have the letters of credit 
provided as security returned to them upon receipt. 
Although the amenity pier will not move forward due to the complexity of water lot 
approvals and engineering requirements, public access to the Fraser River remains in 
place. The existing waterfront trail continues to provide safe and direct access for 
pedestrians and cyclists, and the public will continue to enjoy the river frontage. While 
the pier would have added another feature, the core public access along the foreshore 
is secured. 
The forfeited security will be held by the City and may be applied toward the design or 
construction of a public amenity in the City, consistent with the terms of the covenant. 
Consideration of the future use of these funds will be addressed through the City’s 
capital planning process. 
4.0  COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Not applicable. 
5.0  FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
In accordance with the terms of the Section 219 Covenant, the City will retain the 
security in the amount of $1,363,500. These funds are not currently allocated within the 
2026-2030 Financial Plan, and the City will have some flexibility in how they may be 
used for amenities. The monies received will be deposited into the City’s Capital Asset 
Works Financing Reserve Fund (CAWFF) where they can be utilized for public 
amenities. Consideration of how the funds may be allocated will occur through a future 
capital planning process, at which time Council may determine the most appropriate use 
consistent with the intent of the covenant. 
 
Respectfully submitted, 
E. W. Kozak, General Manager Planning and Development

The City shall be named as an Additional Insured under the Commercial General Insurance. 
4. 
A Cross Liability clause shall be made part of the Commercial General Liability Insurance. 
 
5. 
The Commercial General Liability Insurance shall be endorsed to provide that it will not 
be cancelled, lapsed or adversely changed without at least thirty (30) days written notice to 
the City. 
6. 
The insurance listed above is the minimum requirement. Prior to the payment of any grants 
under this bylaw, the Association shall file with the City a copy of each certificate of 
insurance or, if requested by the City, insurance policy for the insurance that is required 
under this Schedule. All insurance shall be maintained until the end of the term under this 
bylaw, except that coverage for Products and Completed Operations Liability shall be 
maintained for at least twelve (12) months following the term.

briefs.title: Council Appointed Dennis Back as Chief Election Officer for Burnaby's 2026 General Election
briefs.summary: Council approved the appointment of Dennis Back as Chief Election Officer for the 2026 City of Burnaby General Local Election, scheduled for October 17, 2026. The previous officer, Samantha Pellizzari, is on leave and is not expected to return before the election. Back is an experienced administrator who previously served as Burnaby's Municipal Clerk.

briefs.title: Council received report confirming North Road BIA renewal was blocked by property owner opposition
briefs.summary: Council received the Certificate of Sufficiency confirming that the North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) renewal was stopped by a sufficient petition against it. By the March 30, 2026 deadline, 66 property owners — representing 55% of parcels and 52% of total assessed property value — signed opposition letters, clearing the 50% threshold required to block the bylaw. As a result, the BIA renewal bylaw cannot be adopted, and the Association will not receive grant funding for April 1, 2026 to March 31, 2027.
briefs.action_required: If you are a North Road BIA member, contact the Association to learn about next steps for a possible renewal attempt.
briefs.industries: Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council authorized 2026 corporate maintenance for Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation
briefs.summary: Council approved the annual corporate housekeeping steps for the Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation (BHA), the City's wholly owned housing company. This included appointing KPMG as auditor, setting December 12, 2026 as the annual reference date, receiving the BHA's 2025 financial statements, and appointing all 10 board directors to either two- or three-year terms.

key_dates.label: BHA annual reference date — Burnaby Housing Authority Corporation

briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Council received Burnaby Housing Authority's 2025 financial statements and approved 2026 operating budget
briefs.summary: Council received the Burnaby Housing Authority's (BHA) 2025 audited financial statements and 2026 operating budget at its April 14, 2026 meeting. This unlocks $2 million in City operating funding for the BHA in 2026, supporting two housing projects — 204 rental units at 10th Avenue & Newcombe Street and 183 rental townhomes at Marine Drive (Edmonds).
briefs.action_required: If you own property near 10th Ave & Newcombe St or Marine Drive (Edmonds), monitor BHA project updates at burnabyhousingauthority.ca.

key_dates.label: BHA $25 million GIC investment matures — Burnaby Housing Authority

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Finance & Insurance

briefs.title: Council authorized bylaw amendments to launch Burnaby's E-Bike Share and E-Scooter programs
briefs.summary: Council authorized the City Solicitor to bring forward amendments to three Burnaby bylaws — the Street and Traffic Bylaw, the Parks Regulation Bylaw, and the Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw — to support a new E-Bike Share Pilot Program and align with the provincial Electric Kick Scooter Pilot Program already in effect since January 1, 2026. The changes set rules for where e-bikes and e-scooters can be ridden and parked, create designated parking zones for shared e-bikes, and add fines for riding these devices in areas not approved for their use.
briefs.action_required: If you operate near Burnaby parks or streets, check new designated riding and parking zones before using or deploying e-bikes or e-scooters.

briefs.title: Council authorized submission of Burnaby's first Housing Target Progress Report to BC Province
briefs.summary: Council received the six-month interim Housing Target Progress Report and authorized staff to submit it to the BC Ministry of Housing and Municipal Affairs. The report covers September 1, 2025 to February 28, 2026, during which approximately 910 net new housing units were completed in Burnaby — putting the city on track for its Year 1 target of 1,536 units, though slowing construction and paused provincial affordable housing funding raise concerns about future progress.
briefs.action_required: If you develop or invest in Burnaby housing, monitor provincial funding program updates — paused grants may affect project timelines.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Housing Target Order Year 1b report deadline — BC Housing Supply Act

briefs.title: Council Authorized Burnaby's Continued Participation in Building Benchmark BC Year 7
briefs.summary: Council approved Burnaby's continued participation in Building Benchmark BC (BBBC) Year 7, a free voluntary program that helps building owners track and compare their energy use and carbon emissions. The $16,500 annual contribution will be covered within the Planning and Development Department's existing 2026 budget, with no extra funding required.
briefs.action_required: Register your Burnaby building in BBBC Year 7 to access free energy-use and emissions data at buildingbenchmarkbc.ca.

briefs.title: Council authorized a right-of-way for FortisBC natural gas infrastructure at Burnaby Mountain
briefs.summary: Council approved granting a Statutory Right-of-Way (a legal permission allowing a utility to use a strip of land) to FortisBC Energy Inc. over portions of 7950 Pandora St. and 1200 Gaglardi Way. This allows FortisBC to install, operate, and maintain natural gas lines needed to service the Mintara restaurant and other facilities in the Burnaby Mountain Conservation Area. The City received no compensation, as the infrastructure directly benefits City-owned park facilities.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Natural Resources, Food Service

briefs.title: Council Approved $1.35M Contract for Modular Four-Plex Housing Pilot at 8315 14th Avenue
briefs.summary: Council approved a $1,354,463 contract with Echelon Creative Inc. to design, build, and install a modular four-unit residential building at 8315 14th Avenue in Burnaby. The three-storey, factory-built home is a pilot project aimed at testing faster, lower-cost housing construction. The City will own the land and building, which will be rented out as part of Burnaby's residential rental portfolio.
briefs.action_required: If you supply modular construction, trades, or materials, contact Echelon Creative Inc. about subcontracting opportunities.

briefs.title: Council approved a $2.4 million contract to replace Jerry Rogers Creek culvert in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Council approved a contract worth $2,404,076 (including taxes) to HANNA Infrastructure Ltd. to replace an undersized culvert at Jerry Rogers Creek with a larger box culvert built to handle 100-year flood levels under climate change conditions. The project also includes water and sewer utility upgrades. Funding comes from federal gas tax reserves and a drainage development charge reserve fund.

briefs.title: Council Approved $1.36M Contract for Central Valley Greenway Culvert Replacement and Paving
briefs.summary: Council approved a contract worth $1,360,391 (including taxes) to Drake Excavating (2016) Ltd. for the Central Valley Greenway Culvert Replacement and Paving Works. The project covers replacing 16 culverts along the trail between Sperling Avenue and Douglas Road, plus final paving of the trail. Funding comes from TransLink and the City's Capital Asset Works Financing Reserve Fund.
briefs.action_required: If you bid on this contract, note that Drake Excavating (2016) Ltd. was awarded the work.

briefs.title: Council Approved $450,000 Contract Change with Rogers for Cellular Services
briefs.summary: Council approved a $450,000 contract change with Rogers Communications Canada Inc. to keep the City's cellular voice and data services running while Burnaby transitions to a new provider. This is a temporary extension — the City is finalizing a new contract with a different vendor and will bring it to Council for approval at a future meeting.

briefs.title: Council Approved $579,600 Contract Extension for HVAC Maintenance at City Buildings
briefs.summary: Council approved a contract change worth $579,600 (including taxes) with Just Mechanical Ltd. to continue heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) maintenance and repair at various City-owned buildings. This is a temporary extension while Burnaby runs a new competitive bidding process to find a long-term service provider, bringing the total contract value to approximately $5.4 million.
briefs.action_required: If you provide HVAC services, watch for Burnaby's upcoming RFP (public tender) for a new long-term contract.

briefs.title: Council Approved $394,942 Three-Year Renewal for Cisco WebEx Contact Center Licenses
briefs.summary: Council approved a three-year contract renewal with Long View Systems Corporation, worth an estimated $394,942 (taxes included), to keep the City's phone-based customer service system running from May 5, 2026 to May 4, 2029. The Cisco WebEx Contact Center software handles resident phone calls and supports City staff IT help lines. A three-year term was chosen over annual renewals because it costs less overall.

key_dates.label: Cisco WebEx Contact Center contract renewal period begins — Burnaby
Cisco WebEx Contact Center contract renewal period ends — Burnaby

briefs.title: Council Approved $1.67M Contract Renewal for Fire Protection Services at City Facilities
briefs.summary: Council approved a three-year contract renewal with A.W Fireguard Supplies (1991) Ltd. for fire protection system inspections, maintenance, and repairs at City-owned buildings. The contract runs from July 1, 2026, to June 30, 2029, at an estimated cost of $1,666,061 including taxes, with final payment based on actual services delivered.
briefs.action_required: If you supply fire protection services, note this City contract is locked in with A.W Fireguard until June 2029.

key_dates.label: Burnaby fire protection services contract renewal takes effect
Burnaby fire protection services contract renewal expires

briefs.title: Council Approved External Review of Burnaby's Financial Planning and Spending Practices
briefs.summary: Council approved hiring a qualified outside reviewer to examine how the City plans its finances, manages day-to-day spending, and sets priorities for major investments. The reviewer will work with the Financial Management Committee and report findings directly to Council. The decision was driven by falling density bonus revenues (fees paid by developers for extra building height or density) and rising procurement costs, which are making it harder to keep tax increases moderate.
briefs.action_required: Monitor the reviewer's findings — recommendations could lead to changes in City spending or tax levels affecting your business.
briefs.industries: Finance & Insurance, Construction & Development, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council Authorized Rezoning Process for 20-Storey Rental Apartment at 5035 Beresford Street
briefs.summary: Council approved advancing the rezoning application for 5035 Beresford Street (REZ #25-11) to First and Second Reading at a future Council meeting. The proposal would rezone the site — currently a printing shop — to allow a 20-storey, 215-unit rental apartment building in Burnaby's Royal Oak neighbourhood.
briefs.action_required: If you operate near 5035 Beresford Street, watch for public notices about the rezoning bylaw readings.

briefs.title: Council received update on Mayor's Task Force on Burnaby Economic Growth and Resilience
briefs.summary: Council received a progress report on the Mayor's Task Force on Burnaby Economic Growth and Resilience. The Task Force has held five workshops since January 2026, identifying strategic priorities around business support, diversity, and economic resilience. Final recommendations are expected to be presented to Council on June 23, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Task Force's final recommendations to Council on June 23, 2026 — they may affect local business policy.

key_dates.label: Mayor's Task Force final recommendations presented to Burnaby Council

briefs.title: Council received report: Developer forfeits $1.36M pier security, funds held for future public amenity
briefs.summary: Council received a report confirming that Oxford Properties chose to forfeit $1,363,500 in security — money held as a guarantee — instead of building a planned public pier on the Fraser River at Riverbend Business Park. The developer could not get the required provincial approval to use the water lot, so under the terms of a registered covenant (a legal agreement tied to the property title), the funds now go to the City and will be held for a future public amenity project.

briefs.title: Council Adopted Burnaby North Road BIA Bylaw, Authorizing $1.75M Over Five Years
briefs.summary: Council adopted Bylaw No. 14797, renewing the Burnaby North Road Business Improvement Area (BIA) — a defined zone where local businesses pool resources for area improvements — for five years from April 1, 2026 to March 31, 2031. The bylaw authorizes up to $1,755,147.81 in grants to the Burnaby North Road Business Association, funded through a local property tax on commercial and light-industrial properties within the BIA boundary.
briefs.action_required: If your property is in the Burnaby North Road BIA, expect a new local service tax charge on your annual property tax bill.

key_dates.label: Burnaby North Road BIA bylaw term begins — local service tax takes effect
Burnaby North Road BIA — annual budget submission deadline to City
Burnaby North Road BIA — annual audited financial statement due to City
Burnaby North Road BIA bylaw term ends

briefs.industries: Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development
